blocks.text: The anion name determines the suffix in the salt name. The suffix depends on the presence of oxygen and the oxidation state of the central atom.

blocks.title: Binary anions: -ide
blocks.description: Cl⁻ → chloride, S²⁻ → sulfide, Br⁻ → bromide, F⁻ → fluoride, I⁻ → iodide

blocks.title: Oxyanions (max OS): -ate
blocks.rule: Oxygen-containing anion with maximum oxidation state → -ATE
blocks.description: SO₄²⁻ → sulfate, NO₃⁻ → nitrate, CrO₄²⁻ → chromate

blocks.title: Oxyanions (lower OS): -ite
blocks.rule: Oxygen-containing anion with lower oxidation state → -ITE

blocks.title: OS above maximum: per-...-ate
blocks.rule: Oxidation state above typical maximum → prefix PER- + -ATE

blocks.title: OS below minimum: hypo-...-ite
blocks.rule: Oxidation state below typical minimum → prefix HYPO- + -ITE

blocks.text: Mnemonic: no O → -IDE. Has O: max OS → -ATE, lower → -ITE. Higher than max → PER-...-ATE, lower than min → HYPO-...-ITE.
Oxides: {metal/nonmetal} oxide [(valency)]. Examples: Na₂O → sodium oxide; Fe₂O₃ → iron(III) oxide; SO₂ → sulfur(IV) oxide.
Acids: oxyacids are named from their anion. H₂SO₄ → sulfuric acid (sulfate), HNO₃ → nitric acid (nitrate). Binary acids: HCl → hydrochloric acid, H₂S → hydrogen sulfide (hydrosulfuric acid).
Bases: {metal} hydroxide [(valency)]. Examples: NaOH → sodium hydroxide; Fe(OH)₃ → iron(III) hydroxide; Mg(OH)₂ → magnesium hydroxide.
Salts: {anion (-ate/-ite/-ide)} {metal} [(valency)]. Examples: NaCl → sodium chloride; CuSO₄ → copper(II) sulfate; FeCl₃ → iron(III) chloride.
